metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an in-session proving step for the time-to-land metric path: e.g. emit 5 synthetic PutMetricData datapoints (representative durations) during the verification run and assert that `aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --extended-statistics p50 ...` returns a value — so the emission code is exercised without waiting for real traffic.
findings.finding: AC6's time-to-land p50/p90/p99 CloudWatch extended statistics require accumulated real-traffic datapoints over multiple landed changes. On a first-deploy with zero historical data these percentiles are not computable in-session, making this sub-criterion post-sprint-only.
findings.suggested_fix: aws cloudwatch put-metric-data --namespace rebar/host --metric-name time_to_land --values 10 20 30 40 50 --unit Seconds && aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace rebar/host --metric-name time_to_land --extended-statistics p50 --start-time $(date -u -d '5 minutes ago' +%FT%TZ) --end-time $(date -u +%FT%TZ) --period 300 | jq -e '.Datapoints[0].ExtendedStatistics.p50'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'or StatisticValues' from AC6's time-to-land metric spec and require individual datapoints only, since CloudWatch extended statistics (p50/p90/p99) are only available for metrics published as raw individual values, not pre-aggregated StatisticValues records.
findings.finding: AC6 states the time-to-land metric should emit 'individual datapoints, or StatisticValues' and read percentiles via CloudWatch extended statistics. This is an unverified capability assertion: CloudWatch extended statistics (p50/p90/p99) are only available for metrics published as raw individual `Value` datapoints — they are NOT computed from `StatisticValues` aggregated records. Publishing with `StatisticValues` (pre-aggregated SampleCount/Sum/Min/Max) forfeits percentile support entirely, making the 'or StatisticValues' alternative incompatible with the stated p50/p90/p99 objective.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'individual datapoints, or StatisticValues' with 'individual datapoints (one PutMetricData call per landed change, Value=duration_seconds)' — StatisticValues must not be used if percentiles are required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In `monitoring_autolander.tf`, specify `extended_statistic = "p99"` (not `statistic`) for the percentile alarm, confirm that the `PutMetricData` call in `observability.sh` or the loop emitter sets `StorageResolution=1` if sub-minute granularity is needed, and verify the Terraform attribute name against the AWS provider ~>5.0 docs.
findings.finding: AC6 specifies emitting time-to-land duration as individual `PutMetricData` datapoints and reading percentiles (p50/p90/p99) via 'CloudWatch extended statistics'. CloudWatch `GetMetricStatistics` with extended statistics (percentiles) requires that metrics be published with `StorageResolution=1` (high-resolution) OR that the standard-resolution datapoints are present for at least 15 minutes before the percentile calculation is available. The plan does not specify `StorageResolution`, and the Terraform `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` resource uses `extended_statistic` (a separate attribute from `statistic`) for percentile alarms — if the `monitoring_autolander.tf` alarm uses `statistic = "Sum"` (the pattern copied from every existing alarm in the repo) instead of `extended_statistic = "p99"`, the percentile alarm silently falls back to Sum. The plan doesn't specify which alarm attribute to use.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `extended_statistic = "p99"` in the aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resource for the time-to-land alarm, and emit the duration metric with `--storage-resolution 1` in PutMetricData if high-resolution percentiles are needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide the concrete `[capability]` stanza for `All-Projects refs/meta/config` (e.g. `[capability]\n\tcapability-rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`) and the `groups` file entry for the target group, plus a working provisioning script (`setup-all-projects.sh`) that clones `All-Projects` and pushes declaratively — mirroring `setup-project.sh` — so the operator-cutover step is a single idempotent script run, not undocumented manual Gerrit admin UI work.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` as a server-level `[capability]` grant in `All-Projects refs/meta/config` (AC2) and identifies this as a new integration surface with no existing provisioning — 'no `[capability]` block exists yet in the repo'. The plan names the provisioning step ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`') but does not provide the actual `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza or confirm that pushing to `All-Projects refs/meta/config` works with the existing admin SSH key / `setup-project.sh` pattern (which currently only clones and pushes to the `rebar` project, not `All-Projects`). A failed push (wrong group UUID, wrong project ref, or missing admin push permission on All-Projects) silently blocks every rebase the autolander attempts (403 on every `POST /rebase`).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh` that: clones `All-Projects` via SSH (same admin key), fetches `refs/meta/config`, writes a `[capability]` section granting `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader = group Service Users`, resolves the Service Users UUID via `gerrit ls-groups --verbose`, updates the `groups` file, and pushes — idempotent on re-run.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` is a server-level capability granted in `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` under `[capability]`, but no provisioning script or config snippet for this grant is specified, and there is zero prior use of `[capability]` blocks in the repo's `infra/gerrit/` scripts. The `setup-project.sh` only manages the `rebar` project's `refs/meta/config`, not `All-Projects`. This is a new integration surface — Unverified at the config-syntax and provisioning-path level — and a capability gap: the plan names the requirement but does not name which script adds it or provide the exact `[capability]` / `[access]` stanza.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the Docker socket access precondition for the new `docker inspect` probe in `observability.sh` (e.g. confirm the systemd timer unit runs as root or the `docker` group, and add a guard `command -v docker >/dev/null 2>&1 || { logger ...; exit 0; }`) before the probe section.
findings.finding: The `observability.sh` unhealthy feeder for `autolander_errors` (AC3) is specified to poll `docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}'` — a Docker CLI call — but `observability.sh` currently runs as a systemd timer with no Docker socket access (no Docker CLI invocations exist anywhere in the current script). The script runs as root on the host, which typically has socket access, but this precondition is never stated and no existing section in the script uses `docker` at all. If the socket is inaccessible (permissions, daemon down) the probe silently fails or errors, producing no metric emission for the unhealthy state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide and state which provisioning path to use (extend setup-project.sh or create setup-all-projects.sh), and provide the concrete [capability] / [access] stanza so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The Gerrit provisioning path for the All-Projects capability grant is an unresolved OR: 'extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh'. An executor must make a design choice that is architectural (one file vs. a new file with its own invocation wiring).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide and name the specific provisioning file for the All-Projects capability/submit grant (either extend `infra/gerrit/setup-project.sh` or create `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`) and move it from the 'open question' description to the Files section's concrete file list so AC2 has a verifiable artifact.
findings.finding: The Files section leaves the Gerrit provisioning file undetermined ('extend `setup-project.sh` or add `infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh`'), while AC2 treats the All-Projects capability grant as a concrete deliverable that must be verified. This creates a cross-section gap: AC2 specifies what must be provisioned but the Files section does not commit to which file delivers it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC6: declare the status endpoint's access class explicitly — state the exact port binding (e.g., `127.0.0.1:PORT:PORT` for loopback-only, matching existing stack convention), whether nginx will proxy it at a named path (and add that location block to `rebar.conf.template`), and whether it is 'internal-only / VPC-reachable without authn' (with rationale) or 'public-anonymous' (also with rationale) — ambiguity about the access level of a boundary-crossing path is itself a T5c gap.
findings.finding: The AC6 read-only HTTP status endpoint's access posture is undeclared: the plan says 'localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web' without specifying the actual binding (loopback vs Docker-network-published), whether nginx will proxy it (and at what path), or whether any authentication is required — leaving it ambiguous whether the endpoint is internet-reachable and unauthenticated.
findings.location: AC6 — 'Queue-status + liveness surface' / Files section ('exposing the read-only status endpoint per AC6')
findings.suggested_fix: Declare one of: (a) loopback-only binding (`127.0.0.1:PORT:PORT` in docker-compose.yml) with an nginx proxy location `/autolander/status` → `http://127.0.0.1:PORT/` behind TLS (same pattern as `/review/`), access class = 'internal-VPC, no authn required, non-secret queue state' — document rationale; or (b) no port published outside the Docker network, accessible only from sibling containers, with S4's lander_down reading it via the internal Docker network name. Whichever is chosen, add an nginx location block (or explicitly omit one) and annotate the compose port mapping.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal JSON schema (field names + types) for the AC6 status endpoint, at minimum: the heartbeat-age field name, the `wipChain` object key, and the waiting-count field name — so S4 and S5 can be implemented against the same contract.
findings.finding: The AC6 status endpoint's JSON field vocabulary (machine-readable field names) is never defined. The plan describes the payload in prose — 'current `wipChain` (change, phase, `tested_shas`, time-in-phase), the count of `Autosubmit`-set-and-waiting changes, and the heartbeat age' — but never specifies the actual JSON keys. S4's `lander_down` reads the heartbeat age from this endpoint; without a concrete field name (e.g., `heartbeat_age_secs`, `heartbeat_age`) the S4/S5 interface contract is underdetermined and the two stories cannot be implemented independently.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC6 a schema block, e.g.: `{"heartbeat_age_secs": <int>, "wip_chain": {"change_id": …, "phase": …, "tested_shas": {…}, "time_in_phase_secs": <int>}, "queue_depth": <int>}`. This is the minimal surface S4's `lander_down` needs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the counting rule for the `docker inspect`/unhealthy feeder in AC3: poll interval, whether it emits per-transition (edge) or per-interval-while-unhealthy (level), and whether it uses a separate offset file — so the alarm threshold can be calibrated and double-counting with the journald feeder is resolved.
findings.finding: The `autolander_errors` metric combines two feeders with incompatible probe shapes (journald grep vs. `docker inspect` poll), but the plan never specifies the aggregation/counting rule for the `docker inspect` feeder: at what interval it polls, whether it emits a delta count (like the other feeders) or a per-transition count, and whether a hang that simultaneously triggers AUTOLANDER_ERROR in journald AND an `unhealthy` Docker state double-counts into the single metric. This is a decision rule that must be co-located with the metric schema but is absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The unhealthy feeder is edge-triggered: it emits 1 count per `healthy→unhealthy` transition (using `docker events --filter event=health_status --filter event=die` piped to a background listener), stored in its own offset file at `/var/lib/rebar/autolander-unhealthy-offset`. This avoids double-counting with the journald feeder (a hang may write AUTOLANDER_ERROR AND go unhealthy; the journald feeder counts the log line, the unhealthy feeder counts the state transition — both legitimately increment the single alarm metric).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the S3 dependency contradiction in AC7: either add S3 to the Dependencies section (with the specific marker schema/path S5 must write), or reframe AC7's drain outcome as S5-owned state (e.g., a local drain-state file) rather than writing into S3's marker format.
findings.finding: AC7 requires writing 'the S3 outcome marker' during drain-on-shutdown, but the Dependencies section explicitly states S5 is 'independent of the failure/command stories' (which is where S3 outcome markers originate). This creates a cross-section contradiction: S5 both declares independence from S3 and operationally depends on S3's marker schema/interface.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to Dependencies: 'AC7 drain also depends on S3's outcome-marker schema (path + format); S5 writes to that marker during graceful shutdown. The S3 marker path/format must be settled before AC7 is implementable.' Alternatively, if the drain record is purely S5-local, rename it (e.g., 'drain checkpoint file in state volume') to avoid implying S3's schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (e.g. 60 s, consistent with the 15 s poll cadence and the 90 s lander_down threshold) and the corresponding --interval/--retries values in the plan so the Dockerfile author has no design decision to make.
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold inside the container is never assigned a concrete value. AC5 states the poll cadence (15 s) and the S4 lander_down threshold (90 s), but the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK --interval/--timeout/--retries and the 'older than N seconds = unhealthy' comparison value are left implicit. An executor must invent this value.
findings.location: AC4 (HEALTHCHECK hang probe) and AC5 (heartbeat file)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4/AC5: 'HEALTHCHECK --interval=20s --timeout=5s --retries=3; unhealthy if heartbeat mtime > 60 s ago (4× poll, well under the 90 s lander_down threshold).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete port number for the AC6 HTTP status endpoint and resolve the binding scope (localhost-only for same-host agents, or VPC-reachable) so the docker-compose.yml ports stanza and the S4 client URL can be written without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The HTTP status endpoint port is never specified. AC6 requires a 'read-only HTTP status endpoint' that is 'bound so agents can reach it (localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web)' but gives no port number, and the binding scope is also an unresolved OR (localhost/VPC OR behind Gerrit web access).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (derive it: e.g., 3–4× the 15 s poll interval = 45–60 s, with rationale), make it configurable via an env var (e.g., `HEARTBEAT_STALE_SECS`) passed through docker-compose.yml, and add a verification step in AC8 that confirms the chosen value doesn't fire on a healthy bot under normal load.
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold — the age beyond which the heartbeat file is considered stale and the container is marked unhealthy — is never specified in the plan. It is referenced but left undefined, making it an asserted-not-derived default with no measurement or derivation rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Set `HEALTHCHECK_STALE_SECS=45` (3× poll) as a documented default, passed as a build-arg or env var. Document the derivation (3× poll gives two missed beats before alarm). The AC8 end-to-end test should confirm no false-positive unhealthy fires during a normal 2-minute run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (e.g., 45 s = 3× the 15 s poll) and the `--interval`/`--timeout`/`--retries` Dockerfile values in AC4 or AC5, explaining how the chosen value sits between the 15 s poll cadence and the 90 s S4 `lander_down` threshold.
findings.finding: The HEALTHCHECK staleness threshold (the concrete value the Dockerfile `HEALTHCHECK` instruction needs) is never specified. AC4 says the HEALTHCHECK probes 'heartbeat file freshness (stat the AC5 heartbeat in the state volume; unhealthy if older than the staleness threshold)' but never states what 'the staleness threshold' is numerically, nor the `--interval`, `--timeout`, or `--retries` values. AC5 gives two reference numbers (15 s poll cadence, 90 s for S4's `lander_down`) but explicitly choosing a HEALTHCHECK threshold from these anchors is left to the implementer. This is a decision rule whose parameter is referenced but undefined.
findings.location: AC4 — Liveness/restart; AC5 — Heartbeat; Files: infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'HEALTHCHECK threshold = 45 s (3× the 15 s poll cadence); `HEALTHCHECK --interval=20s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD …`. This gives autoheal a chance to restart before S4 declares `lander_down` at 90 s, creating a graduated response: restart attempt at ~45 s, S4 lander_down alarm at 90 s.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the All-Projects refs/meta/config change: capture the current ACL (e.g., `git fetch All-Projects refs/meta/config && git stash`) before applying, document the revert command, and specify whether a staging-Gerrit dry-run is required before production push.
findings.finding: The All-Projects refs/meta/config capability grant has no stated rollback path. The plan acknowledges it as a new provisioning step but does not describe how to revert or back out the grant if it causes unintended ACL effects across other Gerrit projects on the same instance.
findings.location: Files section — `infra/gerrit/` provisioning; AC2 mechanism paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the `infra/gerrit/` provisioning section, add: (1) a pre-step that snapshots the current All-Projects refs/meta/config SHA, (2) the exact revert command (`git revert` or re-push prior SHA), and (3) a note that the grant should be applied on the staging Gerrit instance first (if one exists) before production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure for the Gerrit `All-Projects` capability grant (e.g., 'revert the [capability] stanza commit and push to refs/meta/config as admin') alongside the forward provisioning step in `setup-all-projects.sh` or its documentation.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the Gerrit `All-Projects` `refs/meta/config` capability grant (`rebaseOnBehalfOfUploader` + submit on `refs/heads/*`) is a new admin-only operation, but provides no rollback path. If the autolander misbehaves post-deploy and must be disabled, operators need to know how to revoke these permissions — but the plan only describes the forward provisioning path, not the revert.
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning … setup-all-projects.sh'; AC2 mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'rollback' section to the setup-all-projects.sh script header or a companion runbook entry: 'To revoke: git revert the capability stanza commit and push to All-Projects refs/meta/config; confirm with `ssh gerrit gerrit ls-user-refs --user autolander-bot`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` to the edited-files list with a `/autolander-status/` proxy location block, or explicitly state that the status endpoint is reachable only on the container-internal network (and that S4's callers always run co-located on the same host).
findings.finding: The AC6 status endpoint's network reachability from S4's `land`/`land-status` CLI clients depends on a nginx proxy rule, but `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` (the nginx config) is not named as an edited file in the plan. S4's `lander_down` reads the heartbeat age from this endpoint; if the endpoint is bound only to the container-internal network with no nginx proxy, S4 callers running outside the compose stack cannot reach it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the AC6 status endpoint JSON schema as a named interface contract (in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or a `schema/autolander-status.json` JSON Schema file), including field names, types, and the versioning/evolution strategy (e.g., additive-only, or a `?v=1` path prefix).
findings.finding: The AC6 status endpoint JSON schema — `{wipChain: {change, phase, tested_shas, time-in-phase}, count, heartbeat_age}` — is a new cross-component interface contract that S4's `lander_down` logic depends on, but it is described only inline in AC text with no ADR, design doc, or versioning/evolution strategy.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/autolander-status-api.md` (or equivalent) that records the endpoint path, JSON schema, the S4 consumer contract (specifically the `heartbeat_age` field and the 90 s threshold), and the policy that field removals/renames require coordinated S4 update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to ONE provisioning path for the All-Projects capability grant — prefer extending `setup-project.sh` with a `PROJECT=All-Projects` mode (or a clearly named `--capability` flag) over adding a new `setup-all-projects.sh`, so the established operator runbook pattern has a single entry point.
findings.finding: The plan leaves the All-Projects provisioning path explicitly unresolved — 'extend setup-project.sh or add infra/gerrit/setup-all-projects.sh' — without committing to one approach. This creates config-surface proliferation risk: adding a second project-provisioning script doubles the operator-facing execution surface, whereas setup-project.sh already has 10+ external call-sites in docs and runbooks (two-vote-gate-rollback.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, gerrit-aws-setup.md, adr/0025, adr/0040, etc.) and already handles group creation and idempotent config push. The unresolved fork means the implementer may introduce a new script that could instead be a targeted extension of the existing one.
findings.location: Files section — 'infra/gerrit/ provisioning' paragraph; AC2 mechanism
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `setup-capability.sh` (or extend `setup-project.sh` with `PROJECT=${PROJECT:-rebar}` already in scope at line 26) that, when `PROJECT=All-Projects`, pushes only the `[capability]` stanza to `All-Projects refs/meta/config`. This keeps the SSH/auth/dry-run machinery in one place and lets existing runbooks reference a single script.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC6: specify the binding address and human/admin authentication contract for the status endpoint — either (a) 'binds on 127.0.0.1 only, routed through nginx behind GitHub OAuth, accessible to logged-in Gerrit users', or (b) 'loopback-only, no auth, not reachable outside the host' with an explicit justification, or (c) another named mechanism — so an implementer cannot accidentally expose an unauthenticated port.
findings.finding: AC6 stands up a read-only HTTP status endpoint on the autolander service, but the plan does not state how HUMAN principals (operators, on-call engineers, or users) authenticate to that endpoint — it says only 'bound so agents can reach it (localhost/VPC or behind the same access as the Gerrit web)', which describes a network locality range but is not a named identity mechanism or an explicit no-auth rationale.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to AC6 such as: 'The status endpoint MUST bind on 127.0.0.1 (loopback only) and be proxied through the existing nginx config (which enforces GitHub OAuth for Gerrit UI access), so it is never directly internet-reachable; no separate authentication is added at the endpoint layer.' Or, if loopback-only with no nginx proxy is acceptable: 'The status endpoint binds on 127.0.0.1:PORT only; the host SG does not open this port; no human-facing auth is needed because it is physically unreachable from outside the instance.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC7: Document the operator confirmation path for the emergency-stop sentinel — explicitly state that the AC6 status endpoint (wipChain `phase: paused`) is the intended confirmation signal, and specify the expected error behavior (log line / metric) if the sentinel file cannot be stat'd due to a volume or permissions failure.
findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel interaction (AC7) covers the happy path (create file → bot pauses; remove file → bot resumes) but does not address: (a) how the operator confirms the pause actually took effect vs. a silent failure (e.g., state volume temporarily unmounted, file written to wrong path, permissions error), and (b) what observable signal the operator has if the sentinel is present but the bot has not yet polled and transitioned to `paused`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC7: 'After creating the sentinel, operators confirm the pause by polling the AC6 status endpoint until `phase` shows `paused` (≤ one poll interval, 15 s). If the bot cannot stat the sentinel file (volume error), it MUST log an `AUTOLANDER_ERROR`-tagged line and emit an `autolander_errors` datapoint rather than silently continuing — ensuring the failure is visible in both the AC3 metric and the operator console.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly whether the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` feeder in `observability.sh` uses a simple literal grep (like the `VOTER_ERROR` feeder in section 4) or a multi-clause regex (like the G2P feeder in section 4b), and confirm the concrete grep pattern that will be added.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker … matching the existing `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` pattern' — but reading observability.sh reveals two different probe shapes are lumped under this label: VOTER_ERROR uses a simple literal `grep -cE 'VOTER_ERROR'` (section 4), while the G2P feeder uses a multi-clause regex `G2P_PATTERN` across several log tokens (section 4b). The plan does not specify which shape the AUTOLANDER_ERROR feeder will use, and 'matching the existing pattern' is ambiguous between these two distinct implementations already in the file.
findings.location: Files section / AC3 — 'the `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` journald marker (a structured stdout marker the process itself writes, matching the existing `VOTER_ERROR`/`G2P` pattern)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` feeder uses a simple literal grep — `grep -cE 'AUTOLANDER_ERROR'` on the autolander container's journald — matching the `VOTER_ERROR` section 4 shape (not the multi-clause G2P regex), with an offset-delta file at `/var/lib/rebar/autolander-fail-offset`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout' with a concrete drain deadline value and state the corresponding docker-compose stop_grace_period, or add a pointer to the canonical source of the CI-wait timeout so the executor has a deterministic derivation.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is described as 'e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout' — the 'e.g.' signals an example, not a decision, and the CI-wait timeout value is not stated in this plan or referenced by a canonical source. An executor must independently discover or choose the value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the SIGTERM drain deadline as a concrete value (e.g., `DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECS=300`), expose it as a configurable env var, and ensure `docker-compose.yml` sets `stop_grace_period` to at least `DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECS + 5s` so compose does not SIGKILL before drain completes.
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline is stated only as 'e.g. matched to the CI-wait timeout' — the concrete value is unspecified, and it is unclear whether it is configurable or hardcoded, making it a hardcoded-soon-to-change threshold with no configuration path.
findings.suggested_fix: Set `DRAIN_TIMEOUT_SECS=300` (matching a typical CI-wait timeout) as an env var default; add `stop_grace_period: 310s` in `docker-compose.yml` for the autolander service; reference the CI-wait timeout source of truth so the two stay in sync.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a concrete SIGTERM drain deadline value in AC7 (not 'e.g.') and specify the corresponding docker-compose `stop_grace_period`, explaining the tradeoff (longer = safer for in-flight stacks, slower redeploys).
findings.finding: The SIGTERM drain deadline in AC7 is described with 'e.g.' rather than a fixed value, leaving the decision rule unspecified. AC7 says 'The drain is **time-bounded** (a SIGTERM drain deadline, **e.g.** matched to the CI-wait timeout)'. The 'e.g.' signals the value is illustrative; no concrete deadline is committed. This value must be specified in `stop_grace_period` in docker-compose.yml and in the signal handler — two co-located artifacts that require the same number — but the number is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/autolander/loop.py` (and any new file for the AC6 HTTP status endpoint, if separate) to the Files section under 'Existing files edited' (or 'New files') so all AC5/AC6/AC7 bot-side deliverables have a named file anchor.
findings.finding: The Files section does not list `infra/autolander/loop.py` (or any equivalent bot source file) as an edited file, yet AC5 (heartbeat write on every poll), AC6 (status endpoint + time-to-land metric emission), and AC7 (emergency-stop sentinel check + drain-on-shutdown logic) all require code changes to that module. Three ACs describe bot-side behaviors with no corresponding file in the Files section.
findings.location: Files section (existing files edited) vs AC5, AC6, AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Under 'Existing files edited' add: `infra/autolander/loop.py` — add heartbeat write on each poll iteration (AC5), emergency-stop sentinel stat check (AC7), SIGTERM handler + drain logic (AC7). If the HTTP status endpoint is a separate module, add that as a new file entry too (e.g., `infra/autolander/status_server.py`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a `HEALTHCHECK --start-period` in `Dockerfile.autolander` long enough for the service to complete its first poll and write its initial heartbeat (e.g., `--start-period=60s`), and specify that the HEALTHCHECK treats an absent heartbeat file the same as a stale one only after the start-period expires.
findings.finding: The heartbeat file has a bootstrapping race: the HEALTHCHECK probes heartbeat freshness, but the file does not exist until the first successful poll completes (up to 15 s after container start, plus any startup latency). During this window, `stat` on the absent file will be treated as 'infinitely stale', immediately marking the container `unhealthy` before it has had a chance to run, which triggers autoheal to restart it — creating a restart loop that prevents the service from ever writing its first heartbeat.
findings.location: AC4 (HEALTHCHECK) / AC5 (heartbeat file, 'written on every poll iteration')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4/AC5: 'Dockerfile.autolander sets `HEALTHCHECK --start-period=60s` (≥4× the poll interval) so the container is not evaluated for liveness until after its first expected heartbeat write; the probe script treats a missing file as unhealthy only after start-period.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency model for the AC6 status endpoint's access to the wipChain state (e.g., a read lock, atomic snapshot copy before serialization, or single-threaded async handler that shares the event loop with the poll coroutine).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a read-only HTTP status endpoint (AC6) serving the current wipChain state, but does not specify how concurrent access between the poll loop (writer) and the HTTP handler (reader) is managed. If the poll loop mutates the wipChain dict/object while the HTTP handler is serializing it, the response may contain a torn/partial view or raise a runtime exception.
findings.location: AC6 — 'a read-only HTTP status endpoint on the bot … serving JSON: the current wipChain'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to the Files section and specify how its `BOT_PATHS` (or an equivalent new variable) is extended to include `infra/autolander/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`, so changes to the autolander source trigger a rebuild/restart of the `autolander` compose service.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated consumer that must be updated: it contains a hardcoded BOT_PATHS variable ('src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml') that controls which source changes trigger a container rebuild/restart. The new Dockerfile.autolander and infra/autolander/loop.py paths are absent from BOT_PATHS, so autodeploy will never rebuild or restart the autolander container when its source changes. The plan names autodeploy.sh nowhere in the Files section.
findings.suggested_fix: Add infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh to the edited-files list and describe adding a new AUTOLANDER_PATHS variable (e.g. 'infra/autolander/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander') plus a parallel 'changed "$AUTOLANDER_PATHS"' branch that rebuilds/restarts the autolander service, mirroring the existing BOT_PATHS redeploy logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `autodeploy.sh` with an `AUTOLANDER_PATHS` trigger (analogous to `BOT_PATHS`) covering `infra/autolander/` and `infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander`, so the CD pipeline rebuilds and restarts the autolander service on source changes — add `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` to the plan's edited-files list.
findings.finding: The plan does not extend autodeploy.sh's BOT_PATHS (or add a parallel AUTOLANDER_PATHS) for the new autolander service, so the existing CD pipeline will never rebuild/restart the autolander when infra/autolander/loop.py or Dockerfile.autolander changes after initial deploy. The autolander silently goes stale forever unless manually redeployed.
findings.location: Files section / infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh (not listed as an edited file); AC8 Verification
findings.suggested_fix: Add to autodeploy.sh: AUTOLANDER_PATHS='infra/autolander/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.autolander' and a corresponding 'if changed "$AUTOLANDER_PATHS"' block that rebuilds compose service autolander (same :prev tagging + health-check + rollback pattern as the review-bot block, but probing the AC6 status endpoint instead of /health).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the AC4 rationale: note that `/var/run/docker.sock:ro` does NOT restrict Docker daemon API access (restart/kill remain possible); document the actual mitigations relied upon (e.g. autoheal image pinning, read-only container filesystem, no-new-privileges) so the deploy review can assess the real attack surface.
findings.finding: The plan presents the Docker socket `:ro` mount on the autoheal sidecar as a meaningful security scope ('scoped privilege … a new stack dependency + attack-surface item'), but for a Unix domain socket the 'ro' bind-mount flag only prevents the container from replacing the socket *file* — it does NOT restrict Docker daemon API write operations (POST /containers/{id}/restart, etc.) issued over the socket's IPC channel. The sidecar retains full Docker daemon control despite the :ro annotation.
findings.location: AC4 (Liveness/restart) — 'the sidecar mounts the Docker socket read-only (/var/run/docker.sock:ro) — a scoped privilege noted as a new stack dependency + attack-surface item for the deploy review'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `willfarrell/autoheal` publishes a multi-arch (arm64) image suitable for the AL2023 Graviton (t4g) host (e.g. check `docker manifest inspect willfarrell/autoheal:latest` for arm64 support), and pin a specific digest/tag in docker-compose.yml rather than `:latest` to lock the verified arch.
findings.finding: The plan adopts `willfarrell/autoheal` as a new sidecar dependency but does not verify that the image publishes an arm64 variant suitable for the AL2023 Graviton (t4g) host. The rest of the stack (Gerrit, review-bot) explicitly notes arm64 compatibility; autoheal is introduced without the same check. If the image is amd64-only, every `docker compose up` on the arm64 host will pull an incompatible image and the hang-recovery path (AC4) will silently be absent.
findings.location: AC4 / Files section: docker-compose.yml — willfarrell/autoheal sidecar
findings.suggested_fix: Add `platform: linux/arm64` to the autoheal service entry (or confirm the image manifest includes arm64), and pin to a concrete tag (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.0`) so a future amd64-only publish doesn't break the arm64 host silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either verify that Zuul actually exposes a `resident_time` metric (and that its semantics match queue-wait duration) or drop the citation and justify the time-to-land metric design on its own merits (it is reasonable without the Zuul analogy).
findings.finding: The plan names Zuul's `resident_time` timer as prior art for the time-to-land metric (AC6), but does not verify that this metric actually exists in Zuul under that name or that the CloudWatch implementation approach is analogous. 'Prior art: Zuul resident_time timer' is stated as a justification without confirming the Zuul metric name, semantics, or whether the approach (raw PutMetricData + extended statistics) mirrors what Zuul actually does. This is a minor unverified prior-art citation used to justify the metric design.
findings.location: AC6 — Time-to-land metric paragraph: '(prior art: Zuul resident_time timer)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `willfarrell/autoheal` publishes a `linux/arm64` variant (check Docker Hub manifest or `docker manifest inspect willfarrell/autoheal`) and record the verified digest/tag in the compose service definition; if arm64 is absent, evaluate an alternative (e.g. build autoheal from source or use a platform-native equivalent).
findings.finding: The plan adds `willfarrell/autoheal` as a new compose sidecar but does not verify that the image publishes an arm64 (linux/arm64) variant. The production host is a Graviton t4g (arm64), and `docker compose up` on an amd64-only image either fails hard or runs under QEMU emulation (silently slow, and broken for process-level operations like Docker socket polling). The rest of the stack explicitly tracks arm64 (Dockerfile.reviewbot, gerrit base image, gitleaks install) but the plan is silent on autoheal's architecture.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `platform: linux/arm64` to the autoheal service entry if confirmed supported, or document the verified multi-arch tag (e.g. `willfarrell/autoheal:v0.9.1`) to pin a known-good multi-arch release.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline at least one concrete proving command per AC into the plan (e.g. `docker run --rm ... flock -xn /state/autolander.lock echo ok || echo blocked` for AC1, `curl -s http://localhost:<PORT>/status | jq -e .heartbeat_age` for AC6) rather than deferring all proving commands to an external DD artifact.
findings.finding: The plan defers all concrete proving commands to 'set_verify_commands DD entries' — an external artifact not present in the plan. This means AC8's verification section describes WHAT to verify but provides no executable commands, leaving E6 criterion (a) (every claim has a concrete proving command) unmet within the plan itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the container's behavior under transient Gerrit HTTP failure: define whether the poll loop logs an `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` marker, retries with backoff, or exits, so the autolander_errors metric correctly increments on Gerrit-side errors and operators can distinguish outage from hang.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the deployed container's behavior when Gerrit is transiently unavailable (HTTP 5xx, connection timeout, DNS failure). S2 owns the rebase/submit loop logic, but S5 owns the deployed service's operational posture. There is no specified retry/backoff, error-log emission, or degraded-mode behavior (e.g., sleep-and-retry vs. exit) for the Gerrit HTTP dependency going down, so operators cannot predict whether the container will crash-loop (driving autoheal restarts and metric noise) or silently stall (driving a stale heartbeat and a correct unhealthy signal).
findings.location: AC4 / AC5 / Scope section ('the core rebase/submit loop logic is S2's')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or AC7: 'On Gerrit HTTP error (5xx / connection failure), the poll loop emits an `AUTOLANDER_ERROR` structured log line, sleeps for one backoff interval (e.g., 60 s, capped), and retries — it does NOT exit, so the heartbeat remains fresh and the error is counted in `autolander_errors` without triggering autoheal.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `fetch-secrets.sh` must fail-fast (non-zero exit, error log) when the autolander token SSM fetch fails, and that the compose entrypoint validates `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN` is non-empty before starting the poll loop.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when `fetch-secrets.sh` fails (SSM unavailable, permissions error, network timeout at container start). The token is materialized into a shared `.env`; if the script fails silently or partially, the container starts with an empty or missing `AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN`, then attempts Gerrit calls with a blank credential, producing confusing 401 errors rather than a clear startup failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'fetch-secrets.sh exits non-zero if the SSM GetParameter call fails; the container entrypoint asserts AUTOLANDER_GERRIT_TOKEN is set and non-empty, exiting with a clear error message if absent.'

findings.finding: AC4 says the compose service uses 'autoheal/restart: unless-stopped' to restart on unhealthy — but 'autoheal' (willfarrell/autoheal) is a separate third-party container that must be added to docker-compose.yml. The plan's Files section does not list an autoheal service entry, and the slash notation leaves it unclear whether (a) autoheal is being added, (b) restart: unless-stopped alone is the mechanism, or (c) the executor must choose. This is a real implementation decision with infra consequences.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'infra/compose/docker-compose.yml — add autoheal service (willfarrell/autoheal, monitoring compose-autolander-1)' to the Files section and update AC4 to say 'the willfarrell/autoheal sidecar (added to compose) watches for unhealthy state and restarts the container.'

findings.finding: AC6 specifies a 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric with design-fixed bucket boundaries of 30 s, 1 m, 2 m, 5 m, 10 m, 20 m, 30 m, 1 h' but CloudWatch `PutMetricData` has no native histogram type with named bucket boundaries (unlike Prometheus). An implementer reading this AC literally has no grounded CloudWatch representation to implement. The two viable approaches — publishing individual duration samples with `StatisticValues` (Values+Counts) and using CloudWatch extended statistics (p50/p90/p99), or publishing per-bucket Count metrics — are not mentioned, leaving the mechanism unspecified.
findings.location: AC6 — 'a queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric with design-fixed bucket boundaries of 30 s, 1 m, 2 m, 5 m, 10 m, 20 m, 30 m, 1 h'
findings.suggested_fix: Publish each completed-land duration (in seconds) as an individual `PutMetricData` data point to a `rebar/host:autolander_time_to_land` metric; use CloudWatch extended statistics (p50, p90, p99) in the monitoring TF resource. The 'design-fixed bucket boundaries' are then documentation of the expected distribution shape, not a metric schema.

findings.finding: AC6 specifies 'a queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric with design-fixed bucket boundaries of 30 s, 1 m, 2 m, 5 m, 10 m, 20 m, 30 m, 1 h' published via CloudWatch PutMetricData, but CloudWatch does not natively support histogram metrics with arbitrary fixed bucket boundaries — this is a Prometheus/OpenMetrics concept. CloudWatch `PutMetricData` supports raw values, statistic sets (Min/Max/Sum/SampleCount), and resolution settings, but not bucket-boundary histogram encoding. The plan does not resolve how these bucket boundaries map to CloudWatch's actual API surface.

AC4 specifies a HEALTHCHECK that detects hangs, and AC5 specifies a heartbeat timestamp file updated every 15s poll iteration, but the plan never connects these: the HEALTHCHECK command is never specified as reading/checking the heartbeat file. An executor implementing the HEALTHCHECK has no specification for what it should probe — the decision rule for the HEALTHCHECK is not co-located with the heartbeat schema that the HEALTHCHECK should logically consume.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'The HEALTHCHECK reads the state-volume heartbeat file: `HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD test -f /state/autolander.heartbeat && python3 -c "import os,time,sys; age=time.time()-os.path.getmtime('/state/autolander.heartbeat'); sys.exit(0 if age < 60 else 1)"`. Threshold 60s (4× poll, 1.5× S4 lander_down threshold of 90s) so autoheal fires before S4 declares the bot down.'

findings.finding: AC6 mandates a 'queue-status surface' with design-fixed histogram bucket boundaries but never specifies the emission target: no CloudWatch namespace/metric name, no stdout log format, no state-volume file path, and no HTTP endpoint. Unlike AC3 (`rebar/host:autolander_errors`) and AC5 (heartbeat file in state volume), the AC6 surface has no concrete transport or schema — an executor cannot implement or verify it.

AC7 drain-on-shutdown says 'finish the current `wipChain`, ack its outcome, then exit' but 'ack its outcome' is an undefined protocol: what constitutes an ack? Write a state file? Log a journal marker? Clear the in-memory wipChain and update a persistent record? No mechanism or target is specified, leaving the fallback/completion sub-step of the drain protocol incomplete.

AC7 emergency-stop describes 'a sentinel file / config flag' but never resolves which mechanism is canonical or specifies the sentinel's form: no file path, no config-flag key, no active vs. inactive vocabulary (presence of file? file contents 'true'/'false'? env var?). This is an unresolved enum/vocabulary for the mechanism the poll loop must check each iteration.
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the sentinel-file pattern (consistent with the flock mechanism in AC1): 'The emergency-stop sentinel is a file at `/state/autolander.emergency-stop`; its presence (regardless of contents) pauses the bot. To activate: `docker exec compose-autolander-1 touch /state/autolander.emergency-stop`. To resume: remove the file. The poll loop checks `os.path.exists(SENTINEL_PATH)` at the top of each iteration.'

findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel (AC7) has an undefined CREATE/RETIRE lifecycle: the plan says 'a sentinel file / config flag the poll loop checks' but does not specify where the file lives (which volume path?), who creates it (operator manually? a script?), how an operator triggers it, or whether it is cleaned up after the emergency is resolved.
findings.suggested_fix: Pin the sentinel to the same state volume as the heartbeat (e.g. `/var/lib/autolander/EMERGENCY_STOP`), document the exact `docker exec` or SSM Run Command invocation to create/remove it, and add a monitoring check (e.g. a second `PutMetricData` call in `observability.sh`) that emits a `autolander_paused` metric when the sentinel is present for > 5 minutes.

findings.finding: AC6 mandates a CloudWatch 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric' with design-fixed bucket boundaries. This is a new, more complex metric emission pattern — CloudWatch histogram/percentile support requires publishing statistical sets (--values/--counts or individual data points with timestamps) rather than the simple single-value PutMetricData calls used in every existing observability.sh section. There are zero existing histogram metric emissions in the codebase; all 6+ existing metric sections in observability.sh emit plain Count/Percent scalars. The plan justifies this by 'validate the success criterion,' but a simpler alternative (structured log lines, or a plain Count of completions with percentile approximated from logs) already exists in the codebase pattern.
findings.location: AC6 (Queue-status surface): 'queue-wait / time-to-land histogram metric with design-fixed bucket boundaries of 30 s, 1 m, 2 m, 5 m, 10 m, 20 m, 30 m, 1 h'

findings.finding: The emergency-stop sentinel (AC7) has no defined CREATE/REMOVE lifecycle: the plan doesn't specify who creates the sentinel file to pause the bot, where exactly it lives relative to the state volume mount path, how an operator removes it to resume normal operation, or whether there is any guard preventing the file from persisting across container recreation (which would leave the bot permanently paused after a redeploy).

AC3's second feeder describes detecting the container's `unhealthy` state by grepping journald with `CONTAINER_NAME=compose-autolander-1`, but Docker health-state transitions (healthy→unhealthy) are Docker daemon events — they are NOT emitted as container stdout/stderr lines and will not appear in journald under that CONTAINER_NAME field. The feeder as written will produce zero matches even when the container is unhealthy, making the metric silently incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: In observability.sh's new autolander section, detect the unhealthy state with: `health_status=$(docker inspect -f '{{.State.Health.Status}}' compose-autolander-1 2>/dev/null || echo 'missing')` and publish 1 to `autolander_errors` when `health_status = unhealthy`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
